title: Clovis

jokes.text: Very inspirational speech, ma'am. / Come on, you wrote it, you can't review it.
jokes.setup: Staffer compliments Selina on an 'inspirational speech'
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately calls out that the staffer wrote it, so the compliment is hollow self-praise
jokes.explanation: Sharp opener that immediately establishes Selina's no-BS political persona. The logic is airtight — a writer can't objectively praise their own work — and it's delivered at speed. Efficiently sets the show's tone.

jokes.text: I meant you read it very inspirationally.
jokes.setup: Selina deflates the compliment by noting the staffer wrote the speech
jokes.punchline: The staffer immediately scrambles to a new, slightly desperate compliment that's no better
jokes.explanation: The quick pivot to 'inspirationally' is a tight little cringe beat; the staffer's verbal gymnastics to avoid embarrassment land perfectly in Veep's rhythm.

jokes.text: This is what DC must have been like under Jefferson... except that would have been a horse, right?
jokes.setup: Selina rhapsodizing about Silicon Valley's 'can-do' energy compared to DC
jokes.punchline: Deflates her own historical romanticism by noting Jefferson would've arrived by horse — a bathetic reality check
jokes.explanation: A mild self-deflating historical joke. Works as character texture — Selina's enthusiasm outpacing her intellect — but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: There's a typo on the home page. She might have a 'great sense of density' for this nation, but that's not the line we want to push.
jokes.setup: Staff member discovers the campaign website has a typo — 'density' instead of 'destiny'
jokes.punchline: The deadpan observation that 'density' is not the quality they want to advertise is a sharp political satire beat
jokes.explanation: A great little wordplay beat — 'density' works as both a typo and an accidental character critique of Selina. The line 'that's not the line we want to push' is perfectly dry. Efficient and character-relevant.

jokes.text: You should have changed the name to 'MoneyMoneyMoney.Gov.'
jokes.setup: The team discusses the 'MeetMeyer.com' website as a way for voters to connect with Selina
jokes.punchline: Dan cuts through the PR spin by naming what the site is actually for: fundraising
jokes.explanation: Sharp political cynicism delivered efficiently. Dan cutting through the euphemism of 'MeetMeyer' to expose the fundraising reality is quintessential Veep — but not particularly novel.

jokes.text: 20,000 and Selina sits on your lap.
jokes.setup: Discussion of tiered fundraising: $5k ticket, $10k for the Veep's table
jokes.punchline: Ben escalates the pay-to-play absurdity to its crude logical extreme
jokes.explanation: A crisp escalation joke — the pay-per-access model taken to its transactional nadir. Six words, huge implication. Ben's shamelessness is perfectly in character. The follow-up 'Not really' makes it land even better.

jokes.text: Gary, what are you doing? You look like a newborn giraffe.
jokes.setup: Gary is fanning himself with his arm in the California heat
jokes.punchline: Selina's withering comparison — 'newborn giraffe' — captures his awkward flailing in one precise image
jokes.explanation: Genuinely great insult — 'newborn giraffe' is specific enough to be visual and absurd enough to be funny beyond the obvious. It's perfectly Selina: contemptuous but almost fond. High quotability. The image makes it a rewatch reward.

jokes.text: Wi-Fi. Do you have that in Silicon Valley?
jokes.setup: Selina's team is discussing logistics while walking through Silicon Valley
jokes.punchline: Selina asking if Silicon Valley — the global hub of tech — has Wi-Fi is either oblivious or deadpan; either way, it's funny
jokes.explanation: A mild character joke playing on the politician's tech-naivety vs. the setting. Not sharp enough to be memorable but works as texture.

jokes.text: Oh, you wanna do a 'selfie'? I call that an 'ussy.'
jokes.setup: Someone suggests taking a selfie; Selina explains her own coinage
jokes.punchline: 'Ussy' — apparently her portmanteau for a self-taken photo — sounds like a vulgarity, which she seems unaware of
jokes.explanation: Great character beat: Selina trying to be hip with tech-speak but accidentally coining something crude. 'Ussy' is a funny sound, and her breezy confidence in saying it is the joke. Character-specific and efficient.

jokes.text: She's been really, really slow to develop, actually. They've been fracking in our area.
jokes.setup: Selina asks how old the baby is; mother corrects Selina's guess, mentioning the baby is crawling
jokes.punchline: The mother attributes the baby's developmental delays to fracking contamination — introducing a political controversy in the most personally uncomfortable way possible
jokes.explanation: Superb structural joke. What starts as a cute baby-age conversation pivots into a political crisis in two sentences. The escalation from 'she's slow to develop' to 'fracking' is perfectly timed and darkly funny — a classic Veep ambush. Rewatch bonus for how cleanly the trap springs.

jokes.text: Know what your mommy and me are doing? We're having a very serious conversation about political issues, aren't we? And the issues are complex.
jokes.setup: Selina is being confronted by Cassie about her flip-flop on fracking while holding the baby
jokes.punchline: Selina narrates the awkward political confrontation to the infant in a soothing baby-talk tone, exposing how absurd and trapped she is
jokes.explanation: Beautifully uncomfortable. Selina using baby-talk cadence to describe a political meltdown while holding someone else's potentially fracking-damaged infant is peak Veep cringe. 'The issues are complex' said to a nine-month-old is a quietly perfect line.

jokes.text: We need to make that woman go away. And I don't mean kill her. / Kill her.
jokes.setup: Selina whispers to staff that they need to handle the fracking mother
jokes.punchline: Selina's qualifier 'and I don't mean kill her' is immediately followed by a deadpan 'Kill her' from a staffer, subverting the reassurance
jokes.explanation: Exceptional structural joke — textbook misdirection. Selina pre-empts the dark reading with 'I don't mean kill her,' which should defuse it; the immediate deadpan 'Kill her' from a colleague reinstates it with perfect timing. The economy is almost unimprovable. This is Veep at its best: the disavowal IS the joke.

jokes.text: Excuse me, can I please have my child back? Oh, my God, I've got the baby!
jokes.setup: Selina has been holding Cassie's baby through a political confrontation and staff try to 'wrap it up'
jokes.punchline: Selina has been so absorbed in damage control she forgot she was holding the baby; Cassie has to ask for her child back
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of a politician literally unable to release a baby is a solid gag, and Selina's shocked self-awareness ('I've got the baby!') lands well. Character-specific and earns its moment.

jokes.text: She was like a beached whale with one flipper fucking around in the air, and you were nowhere to push her back in the sea.
jokes.setup: Amy debrief after the fracking mother debacle
jokes.punchline: Amy's description of Selina as a beached whale with one flipper flailing — and blaming a colleague for not saving her — is brutal, vivid, and absurd
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best insults. The extended metaphor of Selina as a beached whale is perfectly sustained — 'one flipper fucking around in the air' is visually specific and grotesque in just the right way. 'You were nowhere to push her back in the sea' extends it without losing the joke. High quotability, very Veep.

jokes.text: That baby had a mean look in its eyes, like its mama.
jokes.setup: Amy criticizes Mike for not helping Selina; Mike defends himself
jokes.punchline: Mike claims he was distracted because the infant had a 'mean look' — an absurd non-excuse that also lands as a Cassie insult
jokes.explanation: Short, funny deflection. The idea of being intimidated by a nine-month-old's stare is absurd enough to work, and 'like its mama' is a clean kicker. Efficient character comedy.

jokes.text: I'm calling this 'Pacific Trim.' You know what 'trim' is? It's twat.
jokes.setup: Mike pitches a West Coast aesthetic theme called 'Pacific Trim' for Selina's look
jokes.punchline: When Selina asks what 'trim' means, Mike's blunt definition — 'It's twat' — creates a comedic crash between political branding and crude slang
jokes.explanation: Extremely Veep. The branding language of politics colliding with crude slang, delivered in the most matter-of-fact way possible. 'It's twat' as a deadpan definition is the joke — no setup needed once you have Mike's breezy confidence.

jokes.text: What about this 'Alcatraz-matazz'? They look like two couches.
jokes.setup: Mike presents hotel room decor options including something he's branded 'Alcatraz-matazz'
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses the elaborate branding by simply observing the furniture looks like two couches
jokes.explanation: 'Alcatraz-matazz' is a funny made-up portmanteau and Selina cutting through it with 'they look like two couches' is the right anti-spin response. Mild but functional.

jokes.text: Remember, while you're there, do not use 'Google' as a verb. They 'Yahoo' things there.
jokes.setup: Amy is prepping Selina for her visit to Yahoo
jokes.punchline: The hyper-specific corporate-culture note — replace 'Google' as a verb with 'Yahoo' — is absurdly bureaucratic and rings painfully true
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational joke about Silicon Valley corporate ego. 'They Yahoo things there' is a perfectly constructed sentence that's funny on its own and funnier knowing the political reality behind it. Efficient and original.

jokes.text: If we were gonna rate it on a scale of one to 'fucked', what would you say?
jokes.setup: Selina's team debriefs on the fracking mother situation
jokes.punchline: Selina's bespoke crisis-rating scale of 'one to fucked' reveals how normalized catastrophe has become in her world
jokes.explanation: A classic Selina line — the casual profanity as a measurement metric speaks to how she genuinely thinks. 'Scale of one to fucked' is a quotable construction that lands because of how matter-of-fact it is. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I lose women and what am I left with? I'm left with gay Latinos and Jews at college, I guess.
jokes.setup: Staff warns that alienating women voters will doom Selina's campaign
jokes.punchline: Selina rattles off her imagined rump coalition in a way that's both politically cynical and blithely tone-deaf
jokes.explanation: A pointed Veep joke about demographic coalition politics said with Selina's signature mix of desperation and obliviousness. The specificity ('Jews at college') makes it funnier and more cutting than a generic complaint would be. Firmly in character.

jokes.text: I've got to take this fucker out. I have to succeed where the Republican Guard failed.
jokes.setup: Selina sees Danny Chung on television undercutting her
jokes.punchline: She compares her political rivalry to Saddam Hussein's military failing to eliminate someone — a wildly disproportionate analogy delivered with total commitment
jokes.explanation: Iannucci's specialty: totalitarian/military language applied to mundane political rivalry. 'Succeed where the Republican Guard failed' is absurdly grandiose and perfectly in character for Selina's self-dramatizing.

jokes.text: It is called 'multitasking,' Dan. I do it every... Damn it. / You just typed the word 'multitasking,' didn't you? / No.
jokes.setup: Selina brags about multitasking while apparently composing a message
jokes.punchline: Dan catches her having literally typed the word 'multitasking' mid-brag, proving she cannot multitask; her denial makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Exquisite structural joke. The self-undermining is perfectly timed — Selina's boast interrupted at exactly the moment it disproves itself. 'Damn it' as the tell, then Dan's deadpan identification, then her reflexive 'No' denial. Three-beat perfection. Classic Veep self-humiliation.

jokes.text: Tame her or shame her?
jokes.setup: Selina asks Dan to deal with the fracking mother
jokes.punchline: Dan's rhyming binary of options — 'tame her or shame her' — reduces the political problem to a brutally cynical either/or while sounding almost playful
jokes.explanation: A genuinely great line. The rhyme makes it feel almost chipper, which deepens the darkness of what's being proposed. Maximum economy — four words, two options, pure Dan. Very quotable and very character-specific to his amoral efficiency.

jokes.text: you were watching a baby fill its diaper.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy argue over who failed to help Selina during the fracking confrontation
jokes.punchline: Dan's counter-accusation that Amy was transfixed by diaper-filling is specific and absurd
jokes.explanation: A decent insult with good specificity. Not as memorable as surrounding jokes but functions well in the rhythm of the argument.

jokes.text: you were nowhere to push her back in the sea... you know, they say all babies are cute, but whoever 'they' are should be stuck in a stroller and pushed into traffic.
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan continue to vent after the Cassie incident
jokes.punchline: Dan's brutal deconstruction of the 'all babies are cute' truism — punishing its proponents with a stroller-into-traffic fate — is delightfully nihilistic
jokes.explanation: Satisfying dark escalation — starting from a gentle platitude and ending with stroller violence. The 'whoever they are' construction is a well-worn setup but the specific punishment fits the crime neatly. Solidly Dan.

jokes.text: Jonah's fake dramatic reading of the fracking incident: 'And that's why drinking chemical sludge is good for you.' / 'But I've been drinking that frack water, and look what it's gone done to my titty milk!' / 'My baby, oh, my baby!'
jokes.setup: Jonah has a viral blog post dramatizing the Selina/fracking mother encounter
jokes.punchline: His mocking re-enactment puts grotesque words in Selina's mouth ('titty milk') and plays Cassie as hysterical — it's hacky, which is exactly the point: Jonah is a bad writer
jokes.explanation: The joke here operates on two levels: the content is absurd and crude, which is intentionally the point because it's Jonah's work. 'Titty milk' is crude enough to get a shock laugh while also characterizing Jonah as a hack. Layered comedy.

jokes.text: 'And that's why drinking chemical sludge is good for you.' 'But I've been drinking that frack water, and look what it's gone done to my titty milk!'
jokes.setup: The team reads Jonah's mock dramatization of the Selina/fracking incident, featuring a cartoon Selina voice
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'titty milk' in a mock political satire — Jonah's version of the story is even more damaging than reality because it's also crude and viral
jokes.explanation: The absurdist escalation of the fracking story into a parody that uses the phrase 'titty milk' is both funny and plot-functional. Jonah's inability to be subtle while being accidentally effective is good character writing.

jokes.text: Sue-ster. Sue of steel. Sue-Sue-Sue-dio. / Jonah, get off the line. And then the planet.
jokes.setup: Jonah calls the VP's secure line and tries various nicknames for Sue
jokes.punchline: Sue's response — 'get off the line. And then the planet' — is a perfect escalation of contempt that goes from dismissal to existential
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. 'Get off the line. And then the planet.' is a landmark Veep insult — the escalation from 'hang up' to 'cease to exist' is perfectly timed, completely in character for Sue's magnificent contempt, and devastatingly efficient. The 'Sue-Sue-Sue-dio' Phil Collins reference makes Jonah's desperation funnier. Among the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: Hey, fucking pencil neck. / He just swallowed his phone in fear.
jokes.setup: Dan asks what to do about Jonah; Kent apparently patches Jonah into the call
jokes.punchline: Kent calls Jonah 'fucking pencil neck' directly to him, then Dan reports that Jonah 'swallowed his phone in fear' — an absurd physical image of Jonah's craven terror
jokes.explanation: The surprise of Kent — the data wonk — suddenly unleashing 'fucking pencil neck' is wonderful; the follow-up image of Jonah literally swallowing his phone adds physical absurdity. Great Kent character subversion.

jokes.text: I'd ask Mike, but he's too happy now that he's married. / Too happy.
jokes.setup: Ben invites Dan to drinks, noting he's leaving out Mike
jokes.punchline: The idea that being 'too happy' disqualifies Mike from after-work drinking is a perfect cynical inversion — happiness is a liability in this world
jokes.explanation: A quiet but sharp joke about the culture of political Washington: sustained happiness is suspicious and alienating. Kent's confirming 'Too happy' as a simple statement of fact makes it better.

jokes.text: It's obvious that I would make the best campaign manager. I'm not asking you to tell her that, but please, would someone fuckin' tell her that?
jokes.setup: Dan confessional/talking head about his campaign manager ambitions
jokes.punchline: The immediate self-contradiction — 'I'm not asking you to tell her' followed immediately by 'would someone tell her' — reveals Dan's ego unraveling in real time
jokes.explanation: Excellent confessional joke. The construction is precise — he pre-emptively denies the ask and then immediately makes the ask. Pure Dan: strategic self-awareness deployed in service of total shamelessness.

jokes.text: Take it from me, Dan — in a couple of months on this job, you'll go from those six-pack abs to this keg. / I haven't seen my penis since the first Gulf War, and I kinda miss the little guy.
jokes.setup: Ben is comparing his physical decline to Dan's current physique, warning him about the job's toll
jokes.punchline: Ben's admission that he hasn't seen his own penis since 1991 — and that he 'kinda misses the little guy' — is both grotesque and unexpectedly tender
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best. The Gulf War timestamp — a specific historical marker for the onset of Ben's weight gain — is a brilliant detail. 'I kinda miss the little guy' adds unexpected pathos to pure vulgarity. The joke works as character-building (Ben as a man consumed by the job), as body humor, and as satire of Washington's corrosive nature. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Well, seems his unit in Iraq did that to some poor slob with a drill. / But that's, you know, just nutfluff.
jokes.setup: Ben is explaining the Danny Chung torture rumor to Dan over drinks
jokes.punchline: Ben describes a horrific torture allegation and immediately dismisses it as 'nutfluff' — an invented diminutive that trivializes the atrocity
jokes.explanation: The word 'nutfluff' doing enormous political work — reducing what sounds like a war crime to something fluffy and dismissible. Ben's nonchalance is the joke. The Veep universe's moral vacuum perfectly captured in a made-up word.

jokes.text: Yeah, and a couple of whisky chasers? / It's still Tuesday for another six minutes.
jokes.setup: They're drinking at what appears to be an early hour
jokes.punchline: The 'still Tuesday' justification — implying there's a day-of-week threshold for whisky chasers, and they're barely within it — is a perfect piece of self-aware rationalization
jokes.explanation: A small, tight joke. The precision of 'six minutes' makes it funnier than 'it's still Tuesday' alone. Works as both a joke about Washington drinking culture and a character moment for Ben.

jokes.text: Mike, I am balls deep in this omelet.
jokes.setup: Selina calls Mike while apparently eating breakfast
jokes.punchline: Selina's description of her relationship with an omelet using the phrase 'balls deep' — crude military/sexual slang applied to breakfast food — is a perfectly absurd piece of self-characterization
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Six words of pure comedic genius. 'Balls deep in this omelet' works because Selina's commitment to the profane idiom is total and the object (an omelet) is completely mundane. It's original, perfectly economical, highly quotable, and could only come from this specific character at this specific moment. A Veep classic.

jokes.text: He just came out of Gary's room. / No. / Yeah. / What is going on with that? / No, he... another room or something.
jokes.setup: Amy notices a man coming out of Gary's hotel room and tells Selina
jokes.punchline: The awkward, non-committal deflection from both characters — nobody wants to name what they're implying — is funnier than any explicit statement would be
jokes.explanation: A cringe-comedy beat carried almost entirely by what isn't said. The cascading denials ('No. / Yeah. / Another room or something') let the audience fill in the gap. Gary's sexuality handled with perfect indirection. Rewatch bonus for picking up the thread.

jokes.text: He's rich, as in wipe-out-the-deficit rich.
jokes.setup: Gary is briefing Selina on Craig Juergensen before the Clovis visit
jokes.punchline: The 'wipe-out-the-deficit' scale of wealth is an absurd but precisely calibrated yardstick that contextualizes Silicon Valley wealth against Washington's frame of reference
jokes.explanation: A solid, efficient descriptor. The deficit is a funny measuring stick for wealth when you're a political figure. Not remarkable, but works.

jokes.text: See, that's wrong. That's too young. No, you shouldn't make your first million until you're in your 30s. That's what Andrew and I did. It kept us completely grounded.
jokes.setup: Gary reveals Craig is 26; Selina reacts to his extraordinary wealth at that age
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'wisdom' that wealth in your 30s keeps you grounded — sharing this as universal advice while clearly living in a bubble of privilege — is perfectly oblivious
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy. Selina's tone-deaf advice about wealth timing — shared as though it's broadly applicable life wisdom — is painfully funny. 'That's what Andrew and I did' as a humble brag frames it perfectly.

jokes.text: Poor kids, they don't realize they're all gonna be executed by the time they turn 30.
jokes.setup: Selina observes young Clovis employees
jokes.punchline: Selina predicts the young tech workers will be 'executed' at 30 — workplace-ageism expressed as a death sentence
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy. 'Executed' is the perfect word choice — overstated but not wrong as a metaphor for Silicon Valley's youth cult. Selina's mix of sympathy and superiority is neatly captured.

jokes.text: You must be 12 years old, then. / I'm not, no.
jokes.setup: Melissa notes she's the oldest person at Clovis, at age 'ancient'
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'you must be 12' quip, and Melissa's literal, humorless denial — 'I'm not, no' — creates a comedy of failed flattery
jokes.explanation: A functional joke. Melissa's flat denial of the compliment subverts the social expectation, but neither line is especially sharp. Works as texture.

jokes.text: Well, I'm really looking forward to meeting Craig. / Absolutely, although it is pronounced 'Cray-eeg.' / 'Creg.' / 'Cray-eeg.' / C-R-A-I-G. / That's right. / 'Creg'? / Uh, 'Cray-eeg.' / 'Cray-eeg.' Very close. / 'Cray-eeg.' That's it. Well done, Amy. / Oh. You got it right, 'Ah-mee.'
jokes.setup: Selina learns Craig's name is pronounced 'Cray-eeg' — a fussy Silicon Valley affectation for a standard name
jokes.punchline: The prolonged inability of Selina (who keeps saying 'Creg') and eventual success of Amy, followed by Melissa retaliating with 'Ah-mee,' is a beautifully escalating name-pronunciation war
jokes.explanation: Brilliant running bit. The joke perfectly satirizes Silicon Valley pretension (insisting on a non-standard pronunciation of a totally standard name) and Selina's inability to accommodate it. The structure escalates beautifully — Amy succeeding where Selina fails, then Melissa's parting shot 'Ah-mee' as payback is a perfect button. This is the episode's best sustained joke construction.

jokes.text: Child care is a huge part of my campaign. / Oh, no, these are Legos. / Craig believes that Legos are an important part of creative thought.
jokes.setup: Selina sees what she assumes are child-care facilities at Clovis and uses them for an on-the-spot campaign plug
jokes.punchline: The facilities are actually Legos for adult employees — Selina's pivot from political talking point to embarrassment is immediate
jokes.explanation: A sharp cringe moment: Selina's politician autopilot (pivot everything to campaign talking points) backfires when the room turns out not to be what she assumed. Good commentary on both Silicon Valley's arrested-development culture and political opportunism.

jokes.text: Oh, God. My brain feels like it's being fucking circumcised.
jokes.setup: Selina is hungover from previous night's drinks with Ben and suffering through whatever noise/situation is happening
jokes.punchline: The visceral, specifically medical description of a hangover as cerebral circumcision is grotesque, precise, and very Selina
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best lines. 'Circumcised' as a hangover metaphor is startling and completely original — it implies removal, sensitivity, and irreversibility. The specificity is what elevates it from generic profanity to genuine wit. High quotability, perfectly Selina. Genuinely exceptional.

jokes.text: Get me a cheeseburger made of aspirin. / Where are you going, Dan? / I am going to get some air and then throw up in it.
jokes.setup: Dan is catastrophically hungover at the poker game
jokes.punchline: 'A cheeseburger made of aspirin' is an absurdist hangover cure; 'get some air and then throw up in it' is a perfect deadpan follow-up that inverts the usual 'get some air' comfort
jokes.explanation: Two excellent lines back to back. 'Cheeseburger made of aspirin' is an absurdist food request with internal logic (cheeseburger = comfort, aspirin = cure). 'Get some air and then throw up in it' inverts the folk remedy perfectly. Dan's physical suffering well-rendered through pure language.

jokes.text: All right, the game is Texas Hold 'Em. There are no comfort breaks in this game. You're gonna sit there and piss your pants like a man while I take your money.
jokes.setup: Jonah is running the poker game with characteristic pomposity
jokes.punchline: 'Piss your pants like a man' is a perfectly absurd piece of masculine bravado — the action (wetting yourself) undermines the stoicism it's meant to demonstrate
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jonah voice. The oxymoronic masculinity of 'piss your pants like a man' is the kind of idiom that sounds almost right until you parse it. Very quotable, perfectly characterizes Jonah's performative aggression.

jokes.text: I got a hangover worse than the film they made after 'The Hangover.'
jokes.setup: Dan arrives at the poker game visibly wrecked
jokes.punchline: The meta-comparison — his hangover is worse than 'The Hangover Part II' (the universally acknowledged inferior sequel) — uses pop culture to describe physical suffering with precision
jokes.explanation: A solid pop culture joke that earns its laugh. The comparison works because it implies exponential badness — the sequel was famously terrible, so exceeding it in awfulness is a meaningful benchmark. Slightly time-stamped but funny.

jokes.text: Sorry, man. His site has traction. / My site has traction. / It was linked on Playbook this morning. / Oh, my God, yeah, did I tell you? My site was linked on Playbook this morning.
jokes.setup: Poker players are talking about Jonah's site having traction; Jonah is present
jokes.punchline: Jonah immediately appropriates the third-person praise into first-person bragging, talking about himself as if he just remembered the good news
jokes.explanation: A good Jonah character beat. He shifts from third-person to first-person mid-conversation, claiming others' words about him as his own discovery. The shameless self-promotion is classic Jonah.

jokes.text: Craig's coding hour is sacred. It happens every day, but you don't know when.
jokes.setup: Selina is waiting for Craig, who is apparently in his 'coding hour'
jokes.punchline: A daily event that happens at an unknowable time is a brilliant satire of Silicon Valley's mystified cult of the founder
jokes.explanation: Exceptional satirical observation. 'It happens every day, but you don't know when' perfectly captures the irrational reverence around tech founders — a repeating, predictable event rendered mystical and unknowable. The Vice President of the United States is made to wait for this. Razor-sharp and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Perhaps if Craig... / It's 'Cray-eeg.' / ...kept some sort of calendar, then he could write down his schedule. Then he would know where he was meant to be.
jokes.setup: Selina, frustrated at waiting, suggests Craig use a calendar — a basic organizational tool
jokes.punchline: Selina's deadpan suggestion that the billionaire tech genius might try using a calendar is both a genuine grievance and a perfect satirical takedown; Melissa interrupting to correct the pronunciation makes Selina's frustration funnier
jokes.explanation: A two-level joke: Selina's withering sarcasm about Craig's poor scheduling, and the Cray-eeg correction landing mid-complaint to further impede her. Both layers work perfectly together. Strong callback to the pronunciation gag.

jokes.text: No, ma'am, that's Ron Jeremy. / I know, he's a great actor. / He was Scar in 'The Lion King.' / That was Jeremy Irons. Ron Jeremy is a porn legend.
jokes.setup: Selina is about to sign Clovis's graffiti wall next to a famous signature; Gary stops her, identifying the name
jokes.punchline: Selina confuses Ron Jeremy with Jeremy Irons — a porn star with a Disney voice actor — which lands in two beats: her misidentification, then her graceful acceptance of the correction ('whatever')
jokes.explanation: Delightful. The Jeremy/Jeremy confusion is the setup, but the payoff is Selina's confident claim that Ron Jeremy is 'Scar from The Lion King' — which is almost impressively wrong. Gary's patient correction and Selina's casual 'whatever' at the end is pitch-perfect character work.

jokes.text: Lance Armstrong. We're having that one chemically expunged.
jokes.setup: Selina and team are looking at the graffiti wall of signatories
jokes.punchline: 'Chemically expunged' — the method chosen to remove Lance Armstrong's signature mirrors how doping was discovered; it's an extremely specific joke about the method of erasure matching Armstrong's transgression
jokes.explanation: Brilliant compact joke. 'Chemically expunged' is the perfect choice because it mirrors Armstrong's chemical scandal — the method of removal matches the method of his downfall. This is very high-craft comedy writing: a joke that rewards attention. Near-maximum economy.

jokes.text: That's your tell, Dan. That's a William fucking Tell. It's like an apple sitting right on your head, Dan. I can see it. It's plain as day. I call.
jokes.setup: Poker game; Jonah identifies Dan's tell while Dan is hungover
jokes.punchline: Jonah extends the poker 'tell' pun into a William Tell metaphor with full-throated enthusiasm — it's both a clever wordplay and a window into Jonah's desperate desire to be funny
jokes.explanation: A decent pun executed with Jonah's signature over-commitment. 'William fucking Tell' is the right level of escalation. The joke is almost better for being a bit much — that's Jonah.

jokes.text: See, I got one pretty lady, and, oh, look... she brought her sister, and... hello, honey. Three pretty ladies, like Saturday night at Chez Jonah.
jokes.setup: Jonah reveals a strong poker hand
jokes.punchline: Jonah's sexually suggestive narration of his cards ('three pretty ladies, like Saturday night at Chez Jonah') implies a rich love life — which the audience knows is entirely fictional
jokes.explanation: A good Jonah character joke. 'Chez Jonah' as a venue name is perfectly self-aggrandizing, and the implication of three women there on a Saturday is pure delusional Jonah bravado. Works because of what we know about him.

jokes.text: Because unlike your boss, his day-to-fuck-up ratio isn't one to one.
jokes.setup: Someone asks why nobody is going after Danny Chung
jokes.punchline: The precise metric of Selina's incompetence — a 1:1 day-to-fuck-up ratio — is a beautifully constructed political insult
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. The KPI-style framing of political incompetence ('day-to-fuck-up ratio') is both analytically funny and devastating. The precision of 'one to one' — not 'two to one' or 'occasional' — implies relentlessness. Peak Veep political comedy. Very quotable.

jokes.text: At least Selina didn't torture any Iraqis. Unless she had an Iraqi maid or something.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Chung's alleged Iraq torture compared to Selina's record
jokes.punchline: Dan's good-faith defense of Selina immediately undermined by his own hypothetical — 'unless she had an Iraqi maid' — suggests he genuinely can't rule it out
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark joke. Dan's defense collapses in one clause. 'Unless she had an Iraqi maid' is funny because (a) it's a specific and weirdly plausible caveat, (b) it implies Selina is someone who might casually mistreat domestic staff, and (c) Dan delivers it as a genuine afterthought. Perfect economy and dark subversion.

jokes.text: Our withdrawal from Afghanistan has been more 'momentarily' than this.
jokes.setup: Melissa keeps saying Craig will be there 'momentarily'; the wait is extending
jokes.punchline: Selina compares the endless 'momentarily' to the drawn-out US withdrawal from Afghanistan — a geopolitical disaster — making Craig's lateness absurdly grandiose
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best. The Afghanistan comparison is unexpected, perfectly scaled (Craig's rudeness = geopolitical debacle), and makes the satire political in the best way. Selina's knowledge of foreign policy applied to petty personal grievance is exactly who she is. The line is nearly airtight — original, structured, earned, economical.

jokes.text: Craig Juergensen, as I live and breathe in the genius.
jokes.setup: Craig finally arrives
jokes.punchline: Kent's over-the-top greeting ('as I live and breathe in the genius') is mock-reverent — the 'in the genius' twist on the idiom is absurd and serves as light mockery
jokes.explanation: A mild joke — 'breathe in the genius' is a slight absurdist twist on a cliché. Works more as character moment for Kent's dry sycophancy than as a strong standalone.

jokes.text: I shouldn't say this because I majored in math, but you have my 1000% attention.
jokes.setup: Craig tells Selina she has his full attention
jokes.punchline: Craig's math-major disclaimer and then immediately saying '1000%' — a mathematically impossible expression — is perfectly absurd Silicon Valley character comedy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant. The self-aware setup ('I majored in math') followed by the mathematically illiterate '1000%' is a perfect character contradiction. Craig is simultaneously aware of precision and completely unable to apply it when excited. This joke could only work with this specific character, and the writing knows it. Among the episode's best.

jokes.text: Anything. Doesn't quite matter. / 'Congress,' 'legislature,' words like that. / Yes. Exactly. What about France? / So pretty. The museums are too big. / They could be smaller. / That's a great point.
jokes.setup: Craig asks Selina to 'talk to him' — about anything
jokes.punchline: Their conversation devolves into: Craig saying French museums could be smaller, Selina agreeing this is a 'great point' — a parody of the meaningless content-free 'conversation' between politicians and tech billionaires
jokes.explanation: Sharply observed satire. The revelation that Craig wants Selina to make mouth-sounds while he codes — and she cheerfully pivots to complimenting his opinion on French museum sizes — captures the emptiness of tech-political alliance perfectly. 'The museums are too big. They could be smaller.' as geopolitical wisdom is delightfully absurd.

jokes.text: If it's a phone and watch, just call it a 'Fotch.' Or a 'Wone.' If you wanted it to bomb.
jokes.setup: Craig demos the Smartch — a smart watch — explaining the portmanteau name
jokes.punchline: Selina invents competing portmanteaus ('Fotch,' 'Wone') and tags 'If you wanted it to bomb' — mocking the naming convention while sounding genuinely helpful
jokes.explanation: A fun bit of Selina wordplay. 'Fotch' and 'Wone' are committed to the bit, and 'if you wanted it to bomb' is a perfectly delivered kicker. Shows Selina's quick wit that often gets buried under her more desperate moments.

jokes.text: The Smartch demo — shaking hands repeatedly failing; 'I feel like we're sawing a tree or something. Now we're milking a cow.' / Maybe there's an 'on' button.
jokes.setup: Craig attempts to demo the handshake-activated Smartch; it fails to work repeatedly
jokes.punchline: Selina's play-by-play of their increasingly desperate hand movements ('sawing a tree,' 'milking a cow') transforms a tech failure into physical comedy; Craig's 'maybe there's an on button' is deflating
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy. The escalating hand metaphors are well-chosen — 'milking a cow' is perfectly undignified for a VP. The joke also skewers Silicon Valley's 'dare to fail' culture by having its prize innovation fail embarrassingly in front of an important guest.

jokes.text: Smartch, can you see for Craig... / Selina, you need to say 'Smartch.' / It's 'Madam Vice President.' / I know! / Okay. Smartch, can you see for Craig... / It's 'Cray-eeg.' / Cray-eeg... the MeetMeyer website. / [shows Sea World opening times]
jokes.setup: Selina attempts to use the Smartch to show her website
jokes.punchline: Interrupted first by the 'Smartch' trigger word correction, then the 'Cray-eeg' pronunciation correction, the Smartch finally responds — with Sea World opening times
jokes.explanation: Masterful escalation. The sequence stacks three separate obstacles (wrong trigger word, wrong name pronunciation, then device failure) and the payoff — Sea World — is perfectly anticlimactic. It calls back the Cray-eeg running gag at exactly the right moment. The structure is near-perfect.

jokes.text: These are the opening times for Sea World. / You know what? Guys, if things work all the time, it means we can't make them better. We have a saying here at Clovis: 'Dare to fail.' / Then that's a job well done.
jokes.setup: The Smartch returns Sea World results instead of MeetMeyer; Craig deploys Clovis philosophy to cover the failure
jokes.punchline: Selina's deadpan 'Then that's a job well done' punctures Craig's Silicon Valley failure-worship with perfect contempt
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the Smartch sequence. Craig's 'Dare to fail' philosophy is standard Silicon Valley inspirational-failure-worship, and Selina's deadpan 'Then that's a job well done' is the perfect political-realist response to tech-optimist nonsense. Efficient, original, and earns every laugh.

jokes.text: Look, if we don't go with this Chung torture story, someone else will. We just don't know the facts. We put it out there, and then something will arrive that backs it up. That's Journalism 101.
jokes.setup: Dan and a colleague debate whether to publish the Chung torture allegation without facts
jokes.punchline: Dan's description of publishing unverified rumors and hoping facts emerge later as 'Journalism 101' is a pitch-black media satire that is also, horrifyingly, recognizable
jokes.explanation: Exceptional media satire. 'That's Journalism 101' as the sign-off on a strategy of 'publish rumor, hope facts catch up' is both darkly funny and painfully true. The confidence with which it's stated is the key. Highly quotable in any media-criticism context.

jokes.text: He's not calling back. Go with it.
jokes.setup: Dan left Jonah a voicemail saying 'call me back if you shouldn't publish it' — and Jonah hasn't called
jokes.punchline: The absence of a call is interpreted as consent to publish a potentially fabricated torture story
jokes.explanation: Brilliant in its economy. Four words ('He's not calling back') followed by three ('Go with it') constitute an entire arc of amoral political logic. Dan has engineered a non-answer as implicit permission for a potentially defamatory story. The simplicity of the delivery is what makes it devastating.

jokes.text: He's not calling back. Go with it.
jokes.setup: After Jonah's voicemail, Dan's colleague notes Jonah set a 'call me back if you disagree' threshold
jokes.punchline: Dan immediately uses the absence of a return call as permission — with total amoral calm — to let the unverified story run
jokes.explanation: Perfect follow-up to Jonah's voicemail. Four words that show Dan operating with completely cynical precision — he exploits the exact loophole Jonah created. The 'Stop.' from someone else adds one more beat. Exceptional economy and structure.

jokes.text: The Smartch learns quickly. It's like it has an Asian mother.
jokes.setup: The Smartch successfully displays MeetMeyer after repeated failures
jokes.punchline: The tech guy attributes the device's rapid learning to having 'an Asian mother' — a racist stereotype delivered with breezy Silicon Valley casualness
jokes.explanation: Below-4 explanation: This is a cheap stereotype joke that relies on a racial cliché rather than any original comedic thinking. The character-integration case (Silicon Valley casual racism) gives it some context, but the joke itself is lazy. The shock of hearing it in a workplace setting is the only gag. Weak by Veep's standards.

jokes.text: College kids made it — it's a comedy thing where they represent you as meat and act out news stories. / It's making fun of our website, which is 'MeetMeyer' — M-E-E-T... / Oh, that's me. What is the site, 'Meeting Meyer'?
jokes.setup: The Smartch accidentally pulls up a parody site where Selina is represented as a literal meat product
jokes.punchline: Selina slowly processes that the parody is of her — the meat-Selina — and then misnames her own website
jokes.explanation: Good multi-layer joke. The 'MeetMeyer/meat Meyer' pun pays off the website setup from the opening. Selina's dawning recognition and then immediately messing up her own website name adds a cringe layer. The meat-as-political-parody is a solid absurdist touch.

jokes.text: Some college kids made it, ma'am. It's a comedy thing where they represent you as meat and act out news stories. It's making fun of our website, 'MeetMeyer'...
jokes.setup: The Smartch finds a parody website; Gary has to explain it to Selina
jokes.punchline: Gary explaining the parody — 'represent you as meat' — while Selina slowly absorbs that her name/website has inspired literal meat puppetry as political satire
jokes.explanation: Good meta-satirical moment. 'They represent you as meat' is a funny sentence that also captures Selina's political dehumanization. The whole sequence escalates well.

jokes.text: Smartch shows pornographic content instead of MeetMeyer — 'Now I'm getting fucked harder that way.'
jokes.setup: After the meat parody, someone tries to correct the Smartch and navigate to the real MeetMeyer
jokes.punchline: The Smartch goes from parody to actual pornography; Selina's 'Now I'm getting fucked harder that way' is a perfect double-entendre about the political and literal situation
jokes.explanation: Peak Veep escalation moment. The Smartch has now progressed: nothing → Sea World → meat parody → pornography. The escalating humiliation lands at maximum public exposure during an official tech-company visit. Selina's line 'Now I'm getting fucked harder that way' works as both a frustrated assessment of the PR disaster and an accidental description of what's on screen. Outstanding economy and structural payoff for a long-running setup. The Ron Jeremy/Jeremy Irons joke pays off here in context. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: If it's any consolation, a porn parody is a sure sign that you've officially made it. / Technically, becoming vice president could count as making it.
jokes.setup: Selina is upset that the Smartch has pulled up an apparent porn parody of her
jokes.punchline: Melissa's consolation that porn parody = success is immediately deflated by Amy's dry observation that the VP title might already count
jokes.explanation: A nicely constructed two-beat joke. Melissa's unintentionally condescending consolation (porn parody = achievement) is immediately punctured by Amy's deadpan observation about the hierarchy of achievements. Clean structure.

jokes.text: So who was that fucking me? Was that Jeremy Irons? / I think you mean Ron Jeremy. / Whatever.
jokes.setup: Selina watches the apparent porn parody of herself on the Smartch
jokes.punchline: Selina again confuses Jeremy Irons and Ron Jeremy — she genuinely cannot keep them straight — and dismisses the correction with 'whatever'
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback. Selina making the same Jeremy Irons/Ron Jeremy error again — specifically in the context of wondering who's in her porn parody — doubles down on the joke perfectly. Her 'whatever' shows she's accepted she'll never get this right. The context (she's asking who's performing with her in a porn) makes it funnier than the original.

jokes.text: Guys, you're not asking me to pull content? That's very much against our ethics. / The political reality is... / I don't follow politics. / But I do like your 'Tablets in the School' initiative. How about I give you a few to get you started? Say, 50,000? / That's a very generous offer. We appreciate that. / Bang. That's how fast we move here.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to get Clovis to remove negative content about her; Craig refuses on ethical grounds
jokes.punchline: Craig's ethics evaporate instantly when he can use a 'charitable donation' (tablets to schools) as a transaction; the speed of the pivot — 'Bang. That's how fast we move here' — is the kicker
jokes.explanation: Superb satire of Silicon Valley's ethical flexibility. Craig's ethics last approximately six seconds before a bribe is offered. 'Bang. That's how fast we move here' as self-congratulation for the speed of his own corruption is a beautifully dark button. The structure — ethical protest, pivot, celebration — is perfectly compressed.

jokes.text: You don't follow politics, I thought. Or you do now follow politics? / We see ourselves as very much post-tax.
jokes.setup: Craig just negotiated a political quid pro quo, but claimed he 'doesn't follow politics'
jokes.punchline: Selina catches the contradiction; Craig's response ('we see ourselves as very much post-tax') is an absurdist deflection that creates a new contradiction
jokes.explanation: Sharp exchange. Selina's 'or you do now follow politics?' catches Craig's convenient amnesia, and 'post-tax' as a philosophical position is a perfect absurdist corporate dodge — implying taxes are simply a concept they've evolved beyond. Very quotable.

jokes.text: You are showcasing other people's content for free. Once the content providers start charging you, do you have a plan for that? / I think there's a misunderstanding. People want to work with us more than they want to be paid. That's a given.
jokes.setup: Selina presses Craig on Clovis's business model of using content for free
jokes.punchline: Craig's response — 'people want to work with us more than they want to be paid' — is presented as obvious ('that's a given'), satirizing Silicon Valley's historic exploitation of content creators
jokes.explanation: Smart satirical exchange. Selina for once is asking the right question, and Craig's answer — delivered with complete sincerity — reveals the exploitation at the heart of platform economics. 'That's a given' as the kicker is perfectly chilling. Anticipates real tech-content debates.

jokes.text: Craig is bookmarking this chat. / He's what? / You mean metaphorically?
jokes.setup: The meeting is wrapping up; Melissa announces Craig's disposition
jokes.punchline: Amy's genuine confusion ('He's what?') and clarifying question ('metaphorically?') punctures the Clovis-speak perfectly
jokes.explanation: Good brief joke skewering tech-speak. 'Bookmarking the chat' is faux-meaningful business language and Amy's deadpan 'metaphorically?' is the right skeptical response. Efficient.

jokes.text: Look at that pile of elbows. / His site's been blowing up all day. Who is that guy? / That's Jonah Ryan. Everybody knows Jonah. / He's well-known in DC circles.
jokes.setup: Craig spots Jonah at the poker game through the internet; the group starts talking about his site
jokes.punchline: 'Pile of elbows' as a physical description of the gangly Jonah is perfect; the group's insistence that 'everybody knows Jonah' and he's 'well-known in DC circles' is deadpan — they both loathe him
jokes.explanation: 'Pile of elbows' is an outstanding physical descriptor for Jonah — grotesque, specific, and visual. The secondary joke of his colleagues technically endorsing him while clearly despising him is a nice deadpan layer.

jokes.text: Okay, how much do we have in the New Venture fund? / 50. / Let's take 3 or 4 and buy that site. / No way! / You mean millions? / Fair enough. Four to six. / He said he's a popular guy, so... / I didn't say that.
jokes.setup: Craig decides on the spot to buy Jonah's site
jokes.punchline: Dan's escalating protestations — 'I didn't say that!' about calling Jonah popular — while Craig casually moves millions around are perfectly timed
jokes.explanation: A good joke about Silicon Valley's casual relationship with enormous sums of money, and Dan's horror at having inadvertently endorsed Jonah. The speed of Craig's decision vs. the magnitude of the sum is the satirical engine.

jokes.text: Destiny. / Clovis wants to buy Ryantology. / That's right! / Oh, my God. You see these brains? See why I'm pattin' 'em? Because they are made of solid platinum.
jokes.setup: Dan reveals to Jonah that Clovis wants to buy his site
jokes.punchline: Jonah's instant grandiosity — 'You see these brains? They are made of solid platinum' while literally patting his own head — is a perfect window into his delusional self-regard
jokes.explanation: Superb Jonah character comedy. 'Solid platinum brains' while self-congratulatorily patting his own skull is visually perfect and entirely in character. The character integration score is high because this could only be Jonah.

jokes.text: Everybody who said that I would never make it... where the fuck are you now, huh? / Some of them are still in senior positions.
jokes.setup: Jonah celebrates his windfall with a triumphant speech about his doubters
jokes.punchline: Dan's dry corrective — 'Some of them are still in senior positions' — deflates Jonah's triumph with the cold truth that his career antagonists are doing fine
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest two-handers. Jonah's victory speech is set up for a classic Dan deflation, and 'Some of them are still in senior positions' is the perfect specificity — not just 'they're fine' but 'they're in power, above you.' Devastating economy. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Fuck you, Kent Davison. Fuck you, Mrs. Gravestock from the third grade. Fuck you, stepdad one and stepdad three.
jokes.setup: Jonah continues his victory speech, naming his personal nemeses
jokes.punchline: The list escalates perfectly: a specific person (Kent), a specific teacher, then the precision of 'stepdad one and stepdad three' — implying stepdad two was fine, or also problematic but not worth addressing
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. The list structure is perfect, and 'stepdad one and stepdad three' is a stroke of genius — the omission of stepdad two is the punchline, implying either a complicated family history or one decent stepparent amidst disasters. The specificity does enormous comedic work. Highly quotable, strong rewatch value, perfect character integration for Jonah. Comparable in quality to the anchor examples.

jokes.text: I think I just had a money-gasm.
jokes.setup: Jonah, overwhelmed by the prospect of wealth, sits down
jokes.punchline: 'Money-gasm' as a portmanteau for financial ecstasy is crude, specific, and perfectly Jonah
jokes.explanation: A great single-line joke. 'Money-gasm' is Jonah's perfect coinage: economical, crude, self-revealing. Three syllables capturing his entire soul. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Jonah with money. God Almighty. It's like if Hitler could fly.
jokes.setup: The team contemplates the implications of Jonah becoming wealthy
jokes.punchline: Dan's analogy — Jonah with money is like Hitler with the power of flight — is a bizarrely specific escalation that both mocks Jonah and reveals how deeply he is loathed
jokes.explanation: One of the all-time great Veep insults. 'It's like if Hitler could fly' is so specifically wrong — the particular superpower of flight attributed to a historical monster — that it transcends the Hitler comparison cliché. It's not 'like Hitler' — it's the terrifying augmented version. The absurd specificity elevates it from dark to genuinely brilliant. Very high quotability, rewatch value, and the kind of line that sticks in memory. Approaches anchor-level quality.

jokes.text: I vote we tape Gary's mouth closed. / I did not say anything.
jokes.setup: Gary apparently said something about the Gary/hotel room man situation in context of Jonah's windfall
jokes.punchline: Amy's extreme response (duct tape) and Gary's wounded denial set up the implication that whatever Gary said was catastrophically indiscreet
jokes.explanation: A decent reaction beat. Works better on rewatch when you understand the Gary subplot. The joke is largely in what's unsaid.

jokes.text: He asked me to come in with him, Jonah did. I'd be rich right now. I'd be handcuffed to a prick, but I'd be rich.
jokes.setup: Dan reveals Jonah once invited him to partner on the site
jokes.punchline: 'Handcuffed to a prick' as a description of business partnership with Jonah is perfectly economical — acknowledging both the upside and the price
jokes.explanation: Strong. 'Handcuffed to a prick' is a memorable phrase — the intimacy of 'handcuffed' vs. the contempt of 'prick' creates a funny cognitive dissonance. Dan's genuine regret makes the joke land harder.

jokes.text: So they buy it for four, so that gives you, what, one? After taxes, it's like 500,000. It's nothing. What are you gonna do with that?
jokes.setup: The team calculates Dan's hypothetical share if he'd partnered with Jonah
jokes.punchline: Amy's dismissal of $500,000 as 'nothing' is a perfect piece of Washington/political class wealth-blindness
jokes.explanation: A good character joke about the warped financial perspective of DC political operators. '$500k is nothing' landing casually is the joke. Functional.

jokes.text: Do you think this is appropriate? 'Cause when you do that to the vice president, this is what happens! / [Gary crashes/injures himself]
jokes.setup: Selina confronts the Clovis staff about something (the porn parody or the Smartch), and Gary apparently gestures or moves dramatically
jokes.punchline: Gary's physical reaction to Selina's indignation causes him to injure himself — inverting the authority of the moment
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that works in the moment but is harder to evaluate from the transcript alone. Gary's self-injury during Selina's attempt at authority is a good slapstick beat. Rewatch bonus for the visual.

jokes.text: I'm taking these people back to dial-up. They think they're kings of America. / Well, in a way they are. / No, they're not. / I take your point.
jokes.setup: Selina vents about Clovis after their visit
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'Well, in a way they are' punctures Selina's defiance; Selina's insistence that they're not, followed by Amy's immediate 'I take your point' (without actually conceding), is a perfect little political social dance
jokes.explanation: Sharp exchange. 'I take your point' without agreeing is a perfect Amy move — managing Selina's ego while privately knowing Amy's original point stands. The underlying truth (yes, they ARE kings of America) gives the joke bite.

jokes.text: You know what? I know you're walking around here like you're C-3PO with a big, brass, shiny erection, but I got news for you. This is kindergarten for cyberbrats.
jokes.setup: Selina reads out a colleague (Kent?) who seems too enthusiastic about Clovis
jokes.punchline: 'C-3PO with a big, brass, shiny erection' is an extraordinarily specific insult combining robot imagery, sexual vulgarity, and material specificity ('brass') into something genuinely original
jokes.explanation: One of the best insults in the episode. 'C-3PO with a big, brass, shiny erection' is absurd on multiple levels: C-3PO is famously genderless/robotic; 'brass' is the right material for the joke (literal, metallic, appropriate to the robot); the whole image is grotesque and weirdly specific. 'Kindergarten for cyberbrats' as a kicker is also strong. Selina's insulting creativity at its peak.

jokes.text: If you're over 30... check it out. That's where you're headed, my friend, if you work here. / I've got to go to the bathroom. / They have a bathroom here, or do they put their turds up in the cloud?
jokes.setup: Selina points out the 'over 30' disposal area at Clovis, then needs the bathroom
jokes.punchline: 'Do they put their turds up in the cloud?' is a perfect condensation of Selina's techno-skepticism — applying cloud computing literally to bodily functions
jokes.explanation: Excellent. 'Do they put their turds up in the cloud?' takes Silicon Valley's most sacred metaphor and subjects it to the most mundane bodily reality. The joke is structurally perfect: the setup (bathroom need) creates the opportunity for the cloud computing reframe. High originality, great economy. Among the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: Aw, it's a space toilet.
jokes.setup: Selina finds the Clovis bathroom
jokes.punchline: The delight in 'it's a space toilet' — said with what appears to be genuine wonder — is funny because it contradicts her immediately preceding disdain for Clovis tech
jokes.explanation: A nice callback beat. Selina's 'cloud turds' skepticism is immediately undercut by her childlike delight at finding a fancy toilet. 'Space toilet' said with wonder after her rant is a perfect character contradiction. Very economical.

jokes.text: Aw, it's a space toilet. [Selina apparently encountering an unusually high-tech bathroom]
jokes.setup: After predicting Clovis might 'cloud their turds,' Selina enters the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Her childlike 'Aw, it's a space toilet' confirms that Clovis's bathroom is indeed technologically extraordinary — she was right to wonder
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. 'Space toilet' said with Selina's specific mix of delight and condescension is a perfect beat. The 'Aw' is key — she can't help being charmed despite herself. Good visual gag (we'd see an elaborate Japanese-style toilet).

jokes.text: I've been watching you and I like what I see. / Would you work here and share an open plan environment with me? / I'm flattered, but the sheer positivity of this place would break me.
jokes.setup: Craig apparently offers Amy a job at Clovis
jokes.punchline: Amy's rejection — 'the sheer positivity of this place would break me' — is a beautifully honest assessment of an Amy-Clovis culture clash, and a perfect character moment
jokes.explanation: Amy's self-knowledge here is the joke and the character beat simultaneously. 'The sheer positivity of this place would break me' is deeply Amy — she operates on negativity, cynicism, and controlled rage. The idea that optimism is the thing that would defeat her is original and perfectly calibrated. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: This is what we'd pay you. / Go fuck yourself. / Sorry, that's the opposite of what I meant to say.
jokes.setup: Craig shows Amy a number on a device — presumably a very high salary offer
jokes.punchline: Amy's instinctive 'Go fuck yourself' — followed by her realizing she meant 'yes' — reveals both the number's enormity and Amy's complete inability to process positive news gracefully
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. 'Go fuck yourself. Sorry, that's the opposite of what I meant to say.' is near-perfect joke construction. It's a misdirection (we expect delight or negotiation), a character revelation (Amy's default mode is hostility), and a perfectly structured correction. The second sentence is one of the funniest apologies in television comedy. High quotability, could only come from Amy, and the economy is unimpeachable. Among the episode's two or three best jokes.

jokes.text: Chung's proud of his beatboxing, but what about the beating and boxing he did in Iraq? Proud of that, 'Guv'nor'?
jokes.setup: Jonah has published his Chung torture story
jokes.punchline: The beatbox/beating+boxing pun is exactly the level of hack journalism Jonah would produce — 'Guv'nor' as an address to Chung (who was in a British-affiliated unit?) is weirdly specific
jokes.explanation: The joke here is that Jonah is a bad writer — the pun is deliberately clunky. Works as character comedy but deliberately mediocre wordplay isn't particularly enjoyable. The 'Guv'nor' oddity is funny in its randomness.

jokes.text: I've got to confess, ma'am, I've been in a lot of pain lately. It's my shoulder. The masseuse at the hotel this morning told me to take it easy... / It was a masseuse. / That makes total sense now. / Who did you think it was? / Kent, it was a masseuse. / Ah... masseuse. I buy that.
jokes.setup: Gary explains his shoulder pain; reveals a man came from his room because he had a masseuse
jokes.punchline: Kent's 'Ah... masseuse. I buy that.' and Amy's/Selina's reactions reveal they all assumed Gary was having a secret liaison — the word 'masseuse' is both the explanation and the comedy
jokes.explanation: A satisfying payoff to the Gary subplot. 'I buy that' from Kent — as if granting grudging credibility — is perfect deadpan. The repeated insistence 'it was a masseuse' and everyone's reactions make Gary's sexuality a sustained source of gentle comedy without being mean. Rewatch bonus for how the callback lands.

jokes.text: Why else would I invite a man into my hotel room? / Well... / Nothing. / Why are you looking at me like that? / Like what? / Nothing. It's a free country. / I know it's a free country. / I had a cousin like that. / Not to split hairs or anything, but it was a man. So, technically, it's a masseur, not a masseuse. / Right. / Whatever makes you happy. / It doesn't matter. / I don't understand what's... / Whatever makes me happy? / It's complicated, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Gary's masseuse explanation prompts increasingly loaded commentary from the group
jokes.punchline: The escalating implications — 'Whatever makes you happy,' 'I had a cousin like that,' the masseur/masseuse technical correction — pile onto Gary in a perfect cringe crescendo, with Kent's 'It's complicated, isn't it?' as the button
jokes.explanation: Excellent sustained cringe comedy. Each new voice adds a layer of well-meaning presumption. 'Whatever makes you happy' is the perfect patronizing dismissal; the masseur/masseuse correction is pedantic comedy gold; Kent's 'It's complicated, isn't it?' closing it all out is a sublime data-analyst-applying-structure-to-human-sexuality gag. Rewatch value high.

jokes.text: Right now, the public is interested in tax relief for tech firms. / Yeah, but... they're still deciding. / There is a lot of interest in the Chung torture story. The public may decide to put it on the Clovis front page after all.
jokes.setup: Kent discusses public interest polling on various topics
jokes.punchline: Kent frames the potential publication of a story about torture as a data-driven decision by 'the public' — dehumanizing editorial judgment into a polling outcome
jokes.explanation: A sharp satirical moment. Kent treating political editorial decisions as neutral public interest metrics — while obliquely threatening to publish the torture story — is deeply dark and perfectly in character. The passive 'the public may decide' framing is chilling and funny.

jokes.text: Oh... / Now, Selina... / Oh... / some people call me 'Madam Vice President.' / But you can call me whatever you want, Craig. / 'Cray-eeg.' / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Craig introduces Selina at the Town Hall and calls her 'Selina'
jokes.punchline: Selina objects, correctly, to being called by her first name — then completely undercuts herself by telling Craig he can 'call her whatever he wants' — and Amy mutters 'Cray-eeg' as a private callback
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback. Selina's insistence on her title collapsing immediately into 'call me whatever you want' reveals her desperate need for Craig's approval. Amy's quiet 'Cray-eeg' is a perfect private running-gag acknowledgment. The structure rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Okay, Smartch, show me the Clovis home page. / [Smartch fails to respond in the desired way]
jokes.setup: Selina uses the Smartch in a public Town Hall setting — where it has already failed multiple times privately
jokes.punchline: The audience watches in anticipation of (and solidarity with) Selina's inevitable Smartch failure playing out in public
jokes.explanation: A strong structural callback. Every failure of the Smartch throughout the episode has built to this public moment. The comedy is in the setup — we know what's coming — and the cringe of watching it happen in front of an audience.

jokes.text: Okay, Smartch, show me the Clovis home page. [long pause before something goes wrong]
jokes.setup: Selina attempts to demonstrate the Smartch at the Town Hall — the highest-stakes possible public setting
jokes.punchline: The pause itself and the audience's anticipation of another Smartch failure is the comedy — we've been trained by Sea World and the pornography incidents to expect disaster
jokes.explanation: The anticipatory dread of the Smartch being used publicly again is strong comedy — the rewatch bonus is high because you know what's coming. This is structural comedy: the setup has done its work and the audience is ahead of the characters.

jokes.text: You know what? Guys, I have to address this. This Danny Chung torture story. I know Governor Chung very well and I can tell you that these allegations are utterly unfounded... the words 'Danny Chung' and 'torture' — they don't belong in the same sentence. They don't. 'Danny Chung'? 'Torture'? Come on.
jokes.setup: Selina goes off-script at the Town Hall to address the Chung torture story, which she/her team planted
jokes.punchline: Selina's increasingly panicked denial — repeating 'they don't belong in the same sentence' while putting them in sentences together repeatedly — is a perfect political self-immolation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's structural high points. Selina insisting 'Danny Chung and torture don't belong in the same sentence' while saying their names together three more times is perfectly written political farce. The logic is airtight: every denial reinforces the association. Her panic is the engine. This is Veep's satirical mission statement.

jokes.text: Well, ma'am, by denying that 'Chung' and 'torture' are connected, everyone now seems to think that, well, 'Chung' and 'torture' are connected. / And I wonder what magician got that little story out there?
jokes.setup: Amy explains the Streisand Effect to Selina post-Town Hall
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'I wonder what magician got that little story out there' — while knowing she authorized the story — is a perfect piece of studied obliviousness
jokes.explanation: Sharp exchange. 'What magician got that story out there?' — playing innocent about a story she propagated — is wonderfully brazen. Amy's knowing deflation of the denial is the right straight-man response.

jokes.text: Melissa, I've had time to think about it now, and... I'm a grown-up. I don't think I'm the right fit here. I wasn't even that into toys when I was a kid.
jokes.setup: Amy turns down the Clovis job offer she received earlier
jokes.punchline: Amy's reason for declining — 'I wasn't even that into toys when I was a kid' — is a reference to Clovis's Lego culture and a beautifully low-key piece of self-knowledge
jokes.explanation: A quietly excellent Amy character moment. 'I wasn't even that into toys when I was a kid' as a professional dealbreaker is absurd but also completely true to Amy — someone who has been serious and joyless since childhood. The callback to Legos is elegant. High character integration.

jokes.text: The Veep doesn't value you. / She's about to promote me to campaign manager. She values me. / That's off the record, by the way.
jokes.setup: Melissa tries to recruit Amy; Amy defends Selina's regard for her
jokes.punchline: Amy adds 'that's off the record, by the way' to a statement that should not be classified — revealing that she's simultaneously protecting Selina's secrets and peddling them
jokes.explanation: A sharp character moment. 'Off the record, by the way' applied to something she's already said out loud is a perfect piece of DC-speak self-parody. Amy's loyalty and her own career ambitions coexisting messily.

jokes.text: I don't need you. I can buy a thousand Amys. / That's fine by me if your idea of 'special' is Jonah Ryan. / Jonah Ryan? We sunsetted that idea minutes ago. We've withdrawn our offer. / Well, 'like.' Sorry, wrong company.
jokes.setup: Melissa threatens Amy that Clovis doesn't need her specifically; Amy points out they were going to hire Jonah instead
jokes.punchline: Melissa's 'we sunsetted that idea' and Amy's 'well, like — sorry, wrong company' is a perfect exchange — Amy mixing up Facebook's 'like' with Clovis, which immediately humanizes her gaffe
jokes.explanation: 'Sorry, wrong company' is a tiny gem — Amy's self-correction lands perfectly and her mix-up humanizes her after a combative exchange. 'Sunsetted' as tech-speak for withdrawing a job offer is also a sharp satirical jab.

jokes.text: Jonah, we didn't get into this for the money. / You take that chicken soup and you shove it up your soul.
jokes.setup: Dan is consoling Jonah about the lost buyout; Jonah is devastated
jokes.punchline: 'Take that chicken soup and shove it up your soul' — twisting 'Chicken Soup for the Soul' (the self-help book series) into a profane dismissal — is a perfect Jonah insult
jokes.explanation: Outstanding line. 'You take that chicken soup and shove it up your soul' is a masterful subversion — the wholesome self-help franchise ('Chicken Soup for the Soul') gets violated by being told to shove it 'up your soul,' which simultaneously bastardizes both the idiomatic 'shove it' and the book's concept of soul-nourishment. The image is vivid and the wordplay is precise. Very high quotability. One of the episode's best. Meets the 8+ bar easily.

jokes.text: Hey, pizza delivery, buddy. I got some meat you can put in your mouth. / You fuckin' asshole! / Hey, want a complimentary soda?
jokes.setup: Jonah ambushes Dan to gloat about losing $4 million
jokes.punchline: Jonah's pizza delivery gambit is juvenile; the 'meat in your mouth' phrasing is inadvertently homoerotic; 'complimentary soda' after Dan's furious reaction is a perfect de-escalation joke
jokes.explanation: A solid Jonah/Dan confrontation. The 'complimentary soda' after Dan's explosion is the best beat — Jonah's attempt to re-establish cheerful hospitality after goading is perfect character work.

jokes.text: Speaking of screwed, I heard you lost four million dollars. / Yeah. / I feel your pain. / I had to dump that 150 on purpose in the game today. / You fucked me, Dan. You fucked me with your face.
jokes.setup: Dan and Jonah argue over the poker game and the Clovis deal
jokes.punchline: 'You fucked me with your face' is an extraordinary piece of profane specificity — Jonah blaming his poker loss on Dan's face as an instrument of sabotage
jokes.explanation: An exceptional Veep insult. 'You fucked me with your face' is the kind of line that sounds almost like sense — the accusation that Dan's face, as a physical object, was used as a tool of violation. It's nonsensical, it's furious, and it's utterly specific to the Dan-Jonah dynamic. Very high quotability. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: In the ladies' restroom, they have some sort of an Internet toilet. I couldn't figure out how to flush it. It's got all these buttons that freak me out, so could you go handle it? Okay. It's the third stall.
jokes.setup: As the episode wraps up, Selina sends Amy on a mission
jokes.punchline: The Vice President of the United States cannot flush a fancy toilet and sends her chief of staff to deal with it — an absurd delegation of the most mundane task imaginable
jokes.explanation: A great episode-closing joke. The callback to the space toilet (which Selina couldn't figure out) pays off the entire Clovis bathroom thread. Selina delegating 'flush my toilet' to Amy — her chief of staff — is a perfect encapsulation of their dynamic and of Selina's character. Strong rewatch payoff.

tmdb_overview: On a fundraising trip in Silicon Valley, Selina is challenged by an anti-fracking mom and visits the campus of Clovis, a tech company with an ambitious young CEO. In D.C., Jonah makes a crude viral video of the Selina-mom encounter, and Dan, with Ben's help, gets an idea for a rumor he can spread against Chung.
insight: Escalation-driven cringe hits 81 points with 82 jokes compressed into 35 minutes.
